item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry if I worded it weirdly, but the intent of the text in the addendum is to state that the same author wrote the messages scribbled on the title pages of 2-5. Not the books themselves. They're printed books with a message written in pen by someone aside from the author on their title pages. I might reword that if other people think it's unclear, though.

I don't know if the Foundation's technology includes some sort of tranquilizer that takes effect quickly enough to be safe to use in that sort of confrontation. It's possible, but I wasn't really taking that into consideration. In my mind, the Foundation had a man with a gun on their hands and the best way to stop that was to shoot him, since tranqs take time to kick in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"linguistic analysis of the text has indicated that each book was written by a different author. As no evidence of a █████ ███████ has been located, the name is most likely a pseudonym."

some confusion here, analysis says each book has a different author but in the addendum you say 2-5 had the same author, and that handwriting analysis proved it

Also lets just shoot the guy who knows about it. i don't know what kind of deadly force he had that the foundation couldn't tranquilize instead of killing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The handwritten note is just too on-the-nose. Really fell flat for me. However, I really liked the rest of it — particularly the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took people's critique into account, found what I thought was a good way to redo this, and have now officially posted the rewrite onto the page. Thanks to everyone who looked this thing over for me in the chat - your input helped a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, everyone is so up in the air, I admit dream details would be equally interesting to me but +1 for effort anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I don't feel as strongly about it as Ostberg, the amnestic did seem inappropriate to me as well.
Yes, this is a minor and relatively undangerous item, but it still fundamentally violates science as we know it. And this guy can apparantly just do that. As an art project. The importance of that knowledge would vastly outweigh the minor costs of indefinite detention or any moral objections to enhanced questioning methods.

But instead the interviewer doesn't even press him particularly hard. Erasing his memory on top of that is like burning the assembly instructions for a perpetual motion machine because they were in French and Spanish but not English.

It's a minor quibble, I still enjoyed the piece as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well put together, an interesting execution, but just not quite enough for a +1. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the interview, and seeing a skip from a non-AWCY anart group (kinda), but the object itself just doesn't do anything for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just doesn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I dislike the article, and agree with tombones above, I suggest leaving it be and focusing on your next one. +18 isn't bad for a first attempt, and "if it isn't broke, don't fix it" isn't a bad bit of advice. Maybe later , when you'll buff up writing-wise you'll be able to revisit this and fix it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I only know it because someone made a shitty Wanderers Library story about it :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ilu Rumy That Chuang Chou about butterflies and dreams is my favorite eeee

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Once upon a time, I, Chuang Chou, dreamt I was a butterfly, fluttering hither and thither, to all intents and purposes a butterfly. I was conscious only of my happiness as a butterfly, unaware that I was Chou. Soon I awaked, and there I was, veritably myself again. Now I do not know whether I was then a man dreaming I was a butterfly, or whether I am now a butterfly, dreaming I am a man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And to clarify, while I've seen Inception, it was the furthest thing from being on my mind while I wrote this. The idea of questioning the boundaries between what is a dream and what is real was, as Cryogenchaos said, definitely not at all invented by Inception, and I don't think I dealt with this article in an especially Inception-ish way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes me sad that the idea of "questioning the reality of dreams" is attributed to Inception nowadays, even though the concept has been around for-fucking-ever. This isn't even something with you specifically, it just seems like you can't bring up the concept without people going "Oh, like in Inception!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're taking issue to the one part that didn't get changed, but didn't actually tell the author that until I made a sarcastic response to your complaint.

Give better advice next time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If reality cannot be proven real, how can we know that our dreams are dreams?

Yeah, that's Inception in a nutshell. Deal with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I respectfully, completely disagree, but I suppose you and I just view the Foundation in two completely different ways. But beyond that, I just wish that the interview log could end differently, like with it being in a present day perspective, and the guy is detained for a future interrogation, or with him mysteriously disappearing. It's all just my opinion, of course, and it's not "bad" the way it is right now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, I always thought that part of the Mr. Deeds narrative was jarring and out of place. I can see the Foundation putting a tail on him for a while and observing what he's up to in case he gets into any more mischief, but for an object like this violence seems unwarranted and unnecessarily resource-intensive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I would say just leave it the way you want it; if you want to include both, then do both. If you want to do only the interview, then do it. If you want to focus more on the dreams, do it.

Basically, this one can be two different scips. It can be the art thing or it can be a dream entity thing, but it's having a hard time because people either want to see one or the other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I originally had an interview with a test subject, instead, but ultimately the problem is that I don't know how to explain what this thing is without having someone go right out and say it. The entity behind this thing only exists in the form of a subjective experience, so there's no way to test that scientifically - only to depict what people say about it.

The interview is there because without it, this thing was just… bland. It's looking like there are two camps of people here: one that likes the people who made this and see them as the hook for the article, and one who'd prefer I eliminate any talk about the creators whatsoever and focus on the dreams. I'm totally up for rewriting this if only I had any clue how to please both camps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IT HAS TO DEAL WITH DREAMS IT MUST BE INCEPTION AND I HATE THAT MOVIE RRGGHH -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, either those people were daft, or your draft's dream descriptions weren't very interesting. The potential hook of this concept is how people react to believing their weird, disjointed, illogical dream narratives are just as valid as reality. Without insight into those experiences, this might as well be a director's cut of Inception that's just 90 minutes of the chemist guy watching people nap next to a briefcase & kvetching to himself about how much of a failure he is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview dialog is stilted, tonally awkward, and adds almost nothing to the article. Having the object's creator blandly explain why he created it (and why it doesn't work as intended) is about the least creative or interesting approach to flesh out this thing's properties. "People becoming unable to distinguish surreal dreams from reality" is something that practically demands examples/test logs. Instead we get an interview with a weak caricature of an avant-garde artist who doesn't understand the concept of "show, don't tell". Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mr. Deeds is an easily-contained safe class SCP and they tore out his organs while he was alive and conscious to find out how he works. And one of the major reasons as to why the Foundation Secures Contains and Protects anomalous objects in the first place is to use them for research, and to find out how and why they function.
Pretty much all incarnations of the Foundation that I have ever read of would go to extreme lengths to find out how to understand and make anomalous items. And besides, the guy is likely to just go out and create more anomalous items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Failed to alter anything? How so? From the point where you critiqued it, the thing went through two drafts that included more or less completely redoing all the addenda, reworking the entire description, and adding all of the information I have about the people who created this thing. Your critique on the forums was just that this was too similar to the machine from Inception - which aside from the dream-connection I'm having trouble seeing - and I'd really prefer something more constructive if you're going so far to say that you detest this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The item is an easily-contained safe-class SCP with a simple effect; in my mind, the Foundation just didn't see torturing the shit out of him as worth it. Could they have done it? Sure. Would figuring out how this guy made it be worth the difficulty? Probably not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noted my detestation of this in the forums, and you failed to alter anything.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting that they interviewed the creator. Not sure if that's good or bad. But the end of the interview is just bizarre. I mean seriously

For the remainder of the interview, Mr. H████ refuses to provide further information relating to SCP-1397 or his involvement with it. Mr. H████ was dosed with Class-A amnestics and released.

WTF! He admitted that he was single handedly responsible! I can understand if the SCP wouldn't be as good if he explained it, but if the Foundation knew that he knew how it functioned they would have gone to any lengths to find the answer. They would have used drugs, chemicals, other SCP, or even torture the shit out of him! You should really give a much better explanation then that! But beyond that, the rest of the SCP was pretty good, but beyond that, I agree with Reject; the dreams should have more details, and it is very similar to something that AWCY would make. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subjects' reports of their experiences differ, except for the presence of unrealistic or "dream-like" events.

Admittedly, it's clearer on a second read-through, but I feel like some clarification/elaboration upon the dream-like elements would do this good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for all the reasons I said in chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really quite good. The interview is excellent, and It's just a interesting item. I like it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Earlier drafts about this actually did have more focus on the dreams themselves, but people thought they were extraneous and wanted more focus on the people who made this when I asked the chat, so for now I think I'm keeping that as-is unless more people say so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What needs to be clarified in the description, specifically?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dialogue's cool, but the Description's somewhat confusing. If it were clarified, I think I could upvote this. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't really do much for me, and I found the interview log iffy, that said, I love that someone asides from me is writing anomalous objects from former Czechoslovakia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, though it kind of reads like an AWCY piece that stopped being AWCY at the last second. I liked it, but not enough to upvote. What I think could help is more detail about the dreams, since they're the most interesting part of the SCP. Overall, for a first try, not bad!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So yeah, just here to say this is my first SCP, inspired by the book I've been reading about Czech surrealism. Objektiv isn't meant to be a GOI or anything; this was their first and only attempt at an anomalous object, and once it went wrong the group kind of sputtered out. Aside from their involvement in this SCP, I imagine they were an ordinary group of Czech surrealist writers/artists.

Fingers crossed that I won't have to write another draft of this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Prooty schweet. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the dialog here. An overall very well done SCP.
